title: The Lies We Live By: You're Enough, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Lies We Live By_ You're Enough (Part 1) - Romans 1_19-22 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: The cultural lie: you are enough, you have what it takes
Romans 1:19-22: what can be known about God is plain to all
Humanity suppresses the truth in unrighteousness (v.18)
Knowing God they did not glorify or thank Him (v.21)
Became futile in their thinking, darkened hearts (v.21)
The lie of self-sufficiency as idolatry replacing God
summary: First part of a lesson on the lie 'you're enough'—the cultural message that self-belief and inner strength are sufficient for life. Expounding Romans 1:19-22, the sermon argues that self-sufficiency is a form of suppressing the truth about God. All people know from creation that God exists and must be glorified, but the lie of self-sufficiency leads to futile thinking, darkened hearts, and ultimately idolatry replacing God with self.
transcript:
## The Lie: "You Are Enough"

We'll continue our series on lies that we live by — things that we willingly, or perhaps unwittingly, accept and live by. We often believe in lies without even knowing that we do. Ideas are packaged in such a way that they look shiny and attractive, and we gravitate toward them. We assume that a concept is true because it is being pushed by everyone around us. We tend to think of truth as dictated by a majority — or we think we should do something because it works. In the United States, there is really only one school of philosophy that was actually native to this land, and that is pragmatism: whatever works determines whether something is right or wrong, whether it is true or not.

And sometimes we believe lies because we want to fit in. Standing for what is true can be tiring. It can bring you down — it can be a real burden, especially in this area of the country where darkness seems to be growing deeper. And sometimes we give in and start living according to lies because we are just plain tired. Another reason we live by lies is that we can be a little too lazy to think things through and just take whatever is offered.

In this series we are trying to challenge lies that have been unwittingly, and sometimes deliberately, embraced by Christians in our culture — specifically American culture and in more liberal places like ours. My desire is for us to have our eyes opened to false notions in our own lives so that we can in turn help others see them too. It's a Matthew 7 sort of thing: Jesus says first take the plank out of your own eye before you go help somebody else with their speck.

A lot of these things are treated as gospel truths in our society: *Follow your heart. Trust your instincts — they never lie. Live your truth. You are enough. Put yourself first. Authenticity is everything. You only live once. God just wants you to be happy. You shouldn't judge. You are the boss of you. It's all about love. Believe in yourself. You're perfect just as you are.* Some of these are just different ways of saying the same thing. But they are lies that have been extracted not just from self-help books but from Christian literature. These things are being sold to Christians and by Christians.

So far, we have looked at "follow your heart" and saw that the heart is corrupt and desperately wicked. We addressed "trust your instincts — they never lie," which is just a different way of saying "follow your heart." We looked at "live your truth" — as if there is such a thing as my truth and your truth. There is the truth, and then there are lies.

Tonight I want to examine another popular claim: *you are enough*.

## The Problem with "You Are Enough"

Rachel Hollis, in her book *Girl, Wash Your Face*, writes: "I studied the gospel and finally grasped the divine knowledge that I am loved and worthy and enough as I am." You read that and expect her to follow up with something about being loved and worthy and enough *because of her union with Christ* — she has been studying the gospel, after all. But as you keep reading, you see that she means exactly what she stated: she is enough in and of herself, just the way she is.

Why spend time on this? Because the message "you are enough" is actually a hopeless message. We all know — at least some of the time — that we are not enough. We have all been in situations where we know we did not measure up, where the task was beyond us, where the pit of despair was too deep to climb out of alone. To be told in that moment, "you're enough, you're sufficient in yourself," when you have been trying to climb out of that pit by yourself — that is a hopeless message. It enslaves people to the false idea that they are responsible to be the mastermind of their own circumstances and future realities, even when they are completely overwhelmed by life. It burdens us with the obligation to be the source of our own joy, contentment, and peace.

Another label for the "you are enough" doctrine is *self-esteem*. In the mid-to-late 20th century, Christians embraced the idea of self-esteem. We were conditioned to think that if we just love ourselves more, everything will get better. And we are starting to reap the harvest of a generation raised on the idea that everyone is a winner, trophies for everybody, everyone deserves to be served. The "you are enough" doctrine is based on the assumption that people are basically good. If that were true, all we would need to do is take a deep dive into our own hearts and tap into all the virtue just waiting to be discovered there. And yet it does not work. A May 2021 survey found that 81% of Americans believed the solution to their problems was simply to be told they are great. That same survey found that over 50% of Americans think they are the greatest person they have ever known. And yet the self-esteem movement cannot explain why, after decades of telling people how great they are, we have more relational breakdown, more selfishness, more inability to work together.

Deep down, we know that humans are not basically good. Parents are keenly aware of it about themselves and their kids. No parent needs to teach a child to lie or to be selfish. All parents have had the universal experience of a child who was 100% uninterested in a toy — until another child wanted to play with it. That is human nature.

## Why "You Are Enough" Fails: The Design of Humanity

The reason the doctrine does not work is the very design of humanity. The Bible teaches that humanity was created in the image of God to live in dependence on him. In Genesis 1:26–28, God is creating humanity — and if you read Genesis 1 carefully, there is a cadence: God creates something, declares it good, and moves on. But when it comes to humanity, something changes. What does God do differently?

He *talks to them.*

God did not speak to the moon or the birds or the waters the way he speaks to the people he is creating. He talks with them. Why? Because they needed God to talk to them. Even before sin, Adam and Eve could not figure out life on their own. They were created to be dependent on God. God had to explain to them who they were and what they were to do with their lives. This is the first instance of personal ministry in human history — and it comes from God to the people he created. The Wonderful Counselor comes to human beings and defines their identity and purpose.

Three reasons why Adam and Eve could not live without this:

### 1. Humanity Was Created to Be Revelation-Receivers

We were given unique abilities — the capacity to hear, understand, and apply God's Word. These abilities were given not primarily to encourage human relationships but to enable worship of God in the vertical sense. They were given so that we could know God and understand him. We do not go to the backyard to have a theological discussion with the pine tree, because the bush brings glory to God just by being there. But humans need God's Word to live fully for his glory.

Our culture tends to think we need help because of something we did or something was done to us. But the Bible confronts us with the fact that our need for help preceded sin. We were created to be dependent on God and to receive revelation from him. To try to live without God's help is to assign ourselves a subhuman existence. The "you are enough" doctrine puts people in a place where they do not need God's Word in their life. But to be human is to need to receive guidance from God.

### 2. Humanity Was Created to Be Interpreters

Humans are thinking beings — *homo sapiens*, the thinking person. We do not just take the bare facts of life and live by them. We are meaning-makers. We live according to our interpretation of facts. Our thinking shapes our emotions, our sense of identity, our view of others, our way of solving problems. And that is why we need a framework — lenses through which to see the world. Only the Word of our Creator can give us the framework that helps us live as the interpreters we were designed to be. To say "you're enough apart from anything God gives you" is a lie.

### 3. Humanity Was Created to Be Worshippers

Some have suggested that a more biblically accurate designation than *homo sapiens* would be *homo adorans* — the worshipping person. We were created so that everything in our lives drew its meaning and purpose from the person, presence, and purpose of God. Because of this design, everything a person does expresses worship. What you do and the way you do it express your desire to serve something. Humans cannot be divided into those who worship and those who do not. If you are human, you worship. Every person who has ever lived has been a worshipper.

The fall has distorted this design, and we are often stealing worship from God and redirecting it toward ourselves. When you say "you are enough," the object of worship becomes the self. C. S. Lewis said that humility is not thinking less of yourself but thinking of yourself less. Do not be consumed with thinking about yourself. Self-worship is the demand to be the center of your own universe. And that is what the self-esteem movement has cultivated for decades — people who demand to be the center of everyone's universe. A universe can only have one center.

Adam and Eve were created to respond to God's revelation in worship — to need God's revelation, to interpret life through it, and to worship God in response to it. So you are not enough. You need God. You need the Lord Jesus Christ in your life.

## What Comes Next

We will stop here for tonight. Next week, Lord willing, we will look at what the Bible actually says about our true condition since the fall — and how we are most certainly not enough. The Bible teaches that people naturally want to get their own way, serve their own desires, and resist letting God be in charge. That is why "you are enough" is so appealing: because we do not want to be dependent on anyone else. And yet we cannot exist apart from God being in charge of our lives.
